summary: I was born in 1955 in Mönchengladbach, a city in northwestern Germany near Holland. Growing up there with my mother, older brother, and sister shaped my early life. The quality of bread in Germany, including Mönchengladbach, deeply influenced me and later inspired my baking in Minnesota.

I met my wife while hitchhiking on a trip to New Zealand, which was a pivotal moment in my life. That journey led to our relationship and eventually starting a family. New Zealand was part of the story that brought us together before we settled in Berlin and later moved to Minnesota.

My wife and I had our first two daughters in Berlin, where I also worked as an architect. I loved my career there before moving to Minnesota in 2000. Berlin was an important chapter in my life, balancing family and work before the big move to the U.S.

In 2000, I moved my family to Minnesota to be closer to my wife's family. It was a big change from Germany and Berlin, especially since I lost my architecture job during the 2009 recession. Minnesota became the place where I started baking bread as a hobby and eventually built my bakery business.

I worked as an architect in Minneapolis until the recession in 2009 cost me my job. Later, Minneapolis became the city where I opened Aki's BreadHaus and connected with a community that appreciated traditional German bread. The local breweries in Minneapolis also helped make our pretzels popular, especially during Oktoberfest.

Before opening my own bakery, I worked out of a shared space in Osseo, Minnesota. It was an important step in growing my bread business after starting at farmers markets and a local church. Osseo helped me gain experience and prepare for the bigger opportunity in Northeast Minneapolis.

I partnered with a co-op in Northeast Minneapolis to open my own bakery, which was a major milestone. The community there embraced my traditional German breads and pretzels, and the space eventually became too small due to our success. Northeast Minneapolis has been the heart of Aki's BreadHaus since May 2014.

I opened Aki's BreadHaus in May 2014 in Northeast Minneapolis to bring authentic German bread to the area. We now bake over fifteen kinds of bread and nearly five hundred loaves weekly, along with soft pretzels that are very popular. The bakery has become a place where German Americans and others find a taste of home, and we continue to grow thanks to their support.

Oktoberfest has been a key event for selling our German soft pretzels, which pair perfectly with beer at local breweries around Minneapolis. We sell almost a thousand pretzels weekly, especially during this festival, bringing a bit of German tradition to Minnesota. Oktoberfest really highlights the connection between my bakery and the community's love for authentic German food.
